pages.text: $(italic)Spice of Life: Classic$() adds one quiet rule to every meal: eat the same food too often and it begins to feed you less. Eat with variety and every plate stays worth the bite.$(br2)Nothing in the game announces this — so it earns a page of its own.
pages.title: A Varied Table

pages.text: $(bold)How it works.$()$(br2)Novus keeps a short record of your recent meals. Each time a food turns up again in that record, the nutrition it restores is cut another step — roughly $(bold)100% → 90% → 75% → 50%$(), and then down toward nothing.$(br2)You never have to do this arithmetic yourself. Every food's tooltip shows its $(bold)current value$() — glance at it before you rely on a meal.

$(bold)A worn-out food feeds you nothing at all.$()$(br2)Not a sliver, not half a shank — zero. Lean on one crop hard enough and it simply stops nourishing you.$(br2)The remedy is time and variety: as a food falls out of your recent-meals record, its value climbs back. Rotate three or four staples and none of them ever bottoms out.
pages.title: Let It Rest

pages.text: $(bold)Stocking a varied larder.$() $(italic)Farmer's Delight$() is the natural answer — its stews, pies, and prepared meals give you a wide rotation for little effort. See $(l:2_hearth_season/wild_edibles_hearth)Wild Edibles & Hearth$().$(br2)Two conveniences come with the system: a $(bold)Wicker Basket$() that carries an assortment of foods together, and a $(bold)Food History Book$() listing what you've eaten lately.
pages.title: Stocking the Larder

pages.text: $(bold)One reliable exception.$()$(br2)$(italic)Dried Kelp$() is exempt — it never decays, no matter how much of it you eat. It is the plain, endless fallback for the days when nothing fresh is at hand. Worth keeping a stack against lean times.
pages.title: The Exception
